column1: # OWASP ZAP rule overrides for the DAST scan.
# Format: <plugin-id>\tIGNORE\t# why this is accepted on this site
# See https://www.zaproxy.org/docs/alerts/ for the plugin registry.
# Honoured by slopstopper's dast:analyze task. Keep the list short and
# justified — each entry is a documented exception to the build gate,
# not a way to silence findings we should be fixing.

column3: # Private IP Disclosure: false positive on tutorial blog posts that reference example local Docker network ranges (172.17.x) and AWS VPC CIDRs (10.x.x.x). The IPs appear in documentation prose, not in headers or actual network traffic. Risk: zero — the production site isn't on any of these networks.

# SRI: GTM script and a couple of older blog-post embeds are cross-domain with unstable hashes. SRI on GTM-served scripts isn't viable — GTM rotates content per container update. Documented in docs/security/README.md.

# COEP header: Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy isn't set site-wide. Setting it to `require-corp` would block all cross-origin resources without explicit CORP headers from those origins (GTM, Klaro, Giscus iframes, social-card previews). Not appropriate for a content site that embeds third-party media. Reconsider if/when SharedArrayBuffer is needed.

# Storable but Non-Cacheable Content: HTML deliberately ships `Cache-Control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate` to prevent post-deploy edge staleness. Content-hashed assets under /_astro/* are cached forever. This isn't a CSP-relaxation finding — it's ZAP flagging the deliberate revalidation pattern.

# CSP script-src/style-src 'unsafe-inline': structural to the site. GTM bootstrap is an inline <script> from BaseHead.astro; Astro's scoped <style> blocks inline at build. Switching to nonce-based CSP needs runtime nonce propagation that @astrojs/cloudflare doesn't support cleanly on statically-rendered pages. Risk is low: every page is built from maintainer-authored markdown, no user input is ever rendered into HTML.

# Source Code Disclosure - SQL: false positive on tutorial blog posts containing SQL code blocks (Databricks/Spark/Cosmos emulator). ZAP's heuristic matches the SQL syntax in <pre><code> blocks as if the application were leaking source code; it isn't.
# Modern Web Application: informational only — ZAP's note that the site uses modern JS framework features. Not an alert.
